coredata.dc:title: Orange emissive carbon dots for fluorescent determination of hypoxanthine in fish
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mou, Zehuai
Gao, Zhijin
Hu, Yaoping
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive determination of hypoxanthine (HX), an indicator reflecting the degradation of meat and fish, is significantly important in monitoring food freshness. Herein, we design a novel sensor consisting of orange emissive carbon dots (O-CDs), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NTBC), and xanthine oxidase (XOD) for fluorescence turn-off detection of HX. O-CDs, possessing a high fluorescence quantum yield of 37%, are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine in sulfuric acid. NTBC can react with HX/XOD-generated H2O2 and O2 – to yield a violet-colored formazan, which remarkably quenches the orange fluorescence of O-CDs through inner filter effect. There is a linearity between the quenching efficiency and HX concentration in the range of 2–250 μM, and the limit of detection is 0.61 μM, lower than those of most reported HX sensors. In addition, the proposed method exhibits excellent selectivity, and can be applied to quantify HX in fish samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Orange emissive carbon dots
Fluorescent determination
Hypoxanthine
Food freshness
Nitrotetrazolium blue chloride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HX in the aquatic products

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of orange-emissive CDs and their combination with nitrotetrazolium blue chloride for fluorescence detection of HX based on xanthine oxidase (XOD) reaction.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the as-synthesized O-CDs. Inset in (a): size distribution of O-CDs. (c) Raman and (d) FTIR spectra of O-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) S2p spectra of O-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of aqueous O-CD suspension. Inset in (a): magnified UV–Vis absorption spectrum of O-CDs in the range of 400–700 nm. Insets in (b): photographs of aqueous O-CD suspension in daylight and under 520 nm excitation.

(a) PL spectra of the O-CD based sensor upon addition of different concentrations of HX (from top to bottom: 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, and 500 μM). (b) fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0–F)/F 0 as a function of HX concentration.
(a) PL and PLE spectra of O-CDs, and absorption spectrum of the formazan product. (b) PL decay profiles of O-CDs in the absence and presence of formazan.
Fluorescence response of the O-CD based sensor toward some potential interferences and HX. The concentrations of all analytes are 250 μM.
The results for HX determination in fish samples using the proposed method and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Orange emissive carbon dots for fluorescent determination of hypoxanthine in fish

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive determination of hypoxanthine (HX), an indicator reflecting the degradation of meat and fish, is significantly important in monitoring food freshness. Herein, we design a novel sensor consisting of orange emissive carbon dots (O-CDs), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NTBC), and xanthine oxidase (XOD) for fluorescence turn-off detection of HX. O-CDs, possessing a high fluorescence quantum yield of 37%, are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine in sulfuric acid. NTBC can react with HX/XOD-generated H2O2 and O2 – to yield a violet-colored formazan, which remarkably quenches the orange fluorescence of O-CDs through inner filter effect. There is a linearity between the quenching efficiency and HX concentration in the range of 2–250 μM, and the limit of detection is 0.61 μM, lower than those of most reported HX sensors. In addition, the proposed method exhibits excellent selectivity, and can be applied to quantify HX in fish samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Freshness monitoring of aquatic products is highly required to ensure the quality of food, motivating the great endeavor of searching for freshness biomarkers and developing facile methods for their detection. Hypoxanthine (HX) is an intermediate of nucleotides, generated by the degradation of adenosine triphosphate and accumulated during the food maturation process, which can be employed as an indicator for the quality control of aquatic products [1,2].

In the past few years, a variety of methods have been proposed for HX determination, including mass spectrometry [3], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], electrochemical assay [5], colorimetry [6,7], and fluorometric analysis [8–10]. Among them, fluorometric analysis shares the intrinsic advantages of simple operation, quick response, and high sensitivity. Recently, Hu and coworkers fabricated an amino-functionalized metal organic framework for fluorescence turn-off detection of HX through dynamic quenching effect [8]. Ng group realized fluorescence turn-on quantification of HX using an organic probe with aggregation induced emission feature [9]. Chen et al. established a HX biosensor consisting of platinum nanoparticles and o-phenylenediamine in fluorescence mode [10]. Despite a few impressive cases, the fluorescence assay of HX is still at early stage. It is very significant to exploit novel fluorescent systems toward trace-level sensing of HX.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained growing attention because of their tunable photoluminescence (PL), high photostability, low toxicity, cheap production, and easy functionalization [11–13]. In particular, the unique fluorescence of CDs makes them ideal candidates in advanced optical applications. Although short-wavelength emissive CDs with bright blue or green PL have been widely reported [14,15], much less attempts succeed in fabricating long-wavelength emissive CDs exhibiting strong fluorescence in the yellow to red window (>550 nm), which severely hampers the practical use in various fields, especially in sensing and bioimaging [16].

Herein, we develop a new synthetic route to fabricate orange emissive CDs (O-CDs) by hydrothermal treatment of 2,3-diaminopyridine in sulfuric acid. The as-prepared O-CDs show an intensive PL at 588 nm with a quantum yield of as high as 37%. Coupled with nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NTBC), O-CDs were utilized to construct a novel fluorescence sensor toward HX based on xanthine oxidase (XOD) reaction (Fig. 1 ). This method is demonstrated to be highly sensitive and selective, and can be applied to quantify HX in fish samples with satisfactory results.

Hypoxanthine (HX), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NTBC), glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, lactic acid, uric acid, and inosine were obtained from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Xanthine oxidase (XOD) was acquired from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. 2,3-Diaminopyridine was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Other chemicals including sulfuric acid and metal salts were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

In brief, 100 mg of 2,3-diaminopyridine was added into a 50-mL Teflon autoclave containing 30 mL of 0.5 M sulfuric acid. After 20-min sonification, the mixture was heated in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h, to produce a deep-red O-CD suspension. For purification, the O-CD suspension was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to collect supernatant, passed through a 0.22-μm membrane filter to remove large particles, and dialyzed (molecular weight cut off 1000 Da) with deionized water for three days to remove small organic molecules. The powder of O-CDs was gathered by lyophilization.

In a typical run, 200 μL of a certain concentration of HX was mixed with 200 μL of 0.4 U mL−1 XOD solution at pH 7.5, and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. After that, 200 μL of 1 mg mL−1 NTBC was added into the above mixture, and incubated at room temperature for another 20 min. Finally, 400 μL of 100 mg mL–1O-CDs was added for fluorescence measurement. The PL spectrum was recorded in the range of 540–800 nm under 520 nm excitation, and the PL intensity at 588 nm was used for quantitative analysis. All the tests were repeated for 3 times.

Tilapia fish was obtained from the local aquatic productmarket. After killing, the fish was left to decay at room temperature for 0, 4, 8, 12, 18, and 24 h. For analysis, 1 g of the sample was added into 50 mL of 10% perchloric acid solution. After incubation at 100 °C for 40 min, HX in fish was extracted. The mixture was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to collect supernatant. The pH was adjusted to 7.5 with NaOH. The following procedures were the same as Section 2.3. The concentrations of HX were also tested by HPLC as references.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2 a shows that the as-prepared materials are dot-shaped and nearly monodispersed with an average diameter of about 2.7 nm. Well-resolved lattice fringes are undetectable in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2b), which is indicative of the noncrystalline nature of the nanodots. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 2c displays a predominant D-band at 1435 cm−1 (related to the disordered sp3 carbon) and a weaker G-band at 1603 cm−1 (associated with the graphite sp2 carbon) [17]. The intensity ratio ID/IG reaches a high value of 1.9, supporting the amorphous structure of O-CDs [18]. In the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum (Fig. 2d), several common stretching vibrations are observed, including O–H/N–H in the range of 3096–3435 cm−1, C = O at 1660 cm−1, C = C/C = N at 1617 and 1575 cm−1, and C–N at 1401 cm−1. A moderate-intensity peak at 763 cm−1 represents the out-plane deformation of aromatic C–H [19]. In addition, the distinct peaks at 1147 and 1086 cm−1 are attributed to SO3H and S = O, respectively, suggesting the functionalization of sulfonic acid group on the surface of O-CDs [20]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements are conducted to further investigate the elemental composition and chemical bonds of the material. In Fig. 3 a, the survey spectrum shows the main signals of C, N, O, and S with atomic concentrations of 46.7%, 16.7%, 29.0% and 7.6%, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks that correspond to C–C/C = C (284.5 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), C–O/C–S (286.5 eV), and C = O/C = N (287.4 eV) bonds [21,22]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) demonstrates the presence of pyridinic N (398.7 eV), primary amines bonded to aromatic carbons (400.0 eV), and quaternary N (400.7 eV) [23]. In the S2p spectrum (Fig. 3d), two sulfur species at 168.3 and 169.5 eV are assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 locations of the SO3H group [24,25]. All the above analyses confirm that O-CDs contain both sp2 conjugated domains and sp3 disordered regions in the core, and bear plentiful surface groups like –OH, –NH2, and –SO3H.

UV–Vis absorption spectrum of aqueous O-CD suspension is shown in Fig. 4 a. In the UV region, two sharp peaks at 247 and 316 nm originate from π–π* transition of aromatic networks and n–π* transition of C = O/C = N bonds, respectively [26]. In the visible range, two weaker shoulders at 527 and 560 nm are related to n–π* transitions of sub-fluorophores on O-CD surface [27]. Unlike the typical photoluminescent nanocarbons, O-CDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL property with an emission peak at 588 nm (Fig. 4b). When excited at 520 nm, the pink-colored O-CD suspension exhibits a vivid orange fluorescence (insets in Fig. 4b). The PL excitation (PLE) spectrum displays a peak at 527 nm. This curve closely resembles the absorption spectrum in the region of 420–540 nm (Fig. S1), demonstrating that the surface sub-fluorophores is responsible for the orange fluorescence [28]. The fluorescence decay curve of O-CDs is monoexponential with a short lifetime of 2.78 ns (Fig. S2), which indicates the single emission center of O-CDs. The absolute quantum yield of O-CDs, determined by an integrating sphere, is improved to be 37% under 520 excitation, much higher than those of reported orange emissive CDs [29–32]. O-CDs possess a good optical stability, as the fluorescence intensity remains nearly unchanged after six-month storage (Fig. S3).

We design a hybrid sensor composed of O-CDs, NTBC, and XOD toward fluorescent determination of HX. The sensing mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 1. Initially, in the help of XOD, HX reacts with O2 to produce uric acid, H2O2 and superoxide (O2 –). Then, H2O2 and O2 – trigger the oxidation of NTBC, yielding a formazan product, which shows a deep violet color. Ultimately, the violet formazan quenches the orange fluorescence of O-CDs. In this case, the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs depends on the HX concentration, and the amount of HX in aquatic products can be monitored by the proposed method.

To attain a superior sensitivity, the parameters influencing the performance of the fluorescent sensor are optimized. HX, XOD, and NTBC are nearly colorless when tested alone. Upon addition of NTBC to a solution containing HX and XOD, a violet color, with a broad absorbance band at 590 nm (Fig. S4), is gradually developed due to the generation of formazan. The absorption at 590 nm reaches a plateau after about 20 min (Fig. S5). So, the reaction time of 20 min is chosen. The pH value affects the activity of XOD and the PL intensity of O-CDs. Fig. S6 shows that the optimal pH is 7.5. The concentrations of XOD and O-CDs are selected as 0.4 U mL−1 and 100 mg mL−1, respectively (Fig. S7 and S8).

Under the optimized conditions established, Fig. 5 a depicts the PL spectra of the sensor in the presence of different concentrations of HX (0–500 μM). It clearly demonstrates that the orange fluorescence of O-CDs could be gradually quenched with the increasing HX concentration. There is a nice linear correlation (R2 = 0.9967) between the quenching efficiency (F 0–F)/F 0 and HX concentration over the range of 2–250 μM (Fig. 5b). The equation can be written as (F 0–F)/F 0 = 0.0025 × C HX (μM) + 0.0011, where F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the sensor at 588 nm in the absence and presence of HX, respectively, and C HX stands for the HX concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 0.61 μM (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), which is lower than those of fluorescent sensors adopting metal organic framework or other materials as probes [8–10].

The mechanism of fluorescence suppression is investigated. Fig. 6 a clearly shows that both the PL and PLE spectra of O-CDs overlap well with the broad absorption band of formazan, meaning that not only the emitting fluorescence but also the excitation light of O-CDs can be seriously absorbed by formazan. Under this circumstance, the fluorescence quenching may originate from inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [33]. We study the fluorescence lifetimes to distinguish the above two possible mechanisms. Fig. 6b depicts the very similar PL decay profiles of O-CDs with and without formazan. After addition of formazan, the fluorescence lifetime of O-CDs keeps nearly constant from 2.78 to 2.76 ns, revealing that the quenching is not initiated by FRET but from IFE, because FRET would significantly reduce the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescer, but in the IFE process, the fluorescence lifetime is almost unchanged [34].

To evaluate the specificity of the sensor toward HX, several potential interfering substances, including common metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, and Al3+) and typical biomolecules (glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, lactic acid, uric acid, and inosine), are investigated using the proposed method under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 , these substances cause negligible fluorescence quenching effects, demonstrating the good specificity of the sensor toward HX. Table S1 provides the comparison of different methods for HX detection [5,7–10]. Apparently, the present one is highly sensitive and selective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HX in the aquatic products
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability, the proposed sensor is employed to determinate the amount of HX accumulated in fish samples after death, which reflects the fish freshness. The data obtained by HPLC are used as comparisons. It can be seen in Table 1 that after killing of fish, the HX concentration, monitored by the fluorescent sensor, increases from 2.17 to 6.85 μmol g−1 fish within 24 h. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) are small, with the values less than 7.5%. These results are close to the HX levels detected by HPLC, demonstrating the great potential of the proposed sensor for HX analysis in real samples.

In summary, we have developed a facile hydrothermal method to synthesize orange-emissive CDs with a high QY of 37%. Based on efficient IFE, these fluorescent nanodots are combined with NBTC to determinate HX through an XOD-catalyzed reaction. The proposed HX sensor possesses several advantages including wide linearity, low LOD, and excellent specificity, which has practical applications in accurately monitoring HX in the aquatic products. Our work provides a novel model to design fluorescent sensor for food freshness evaluation.
Zehuai Mou: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Zhijin Gao: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2021J075), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120734.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in nanomaterial-based electrochemical and optical sensors for hypoxanthine and xanthine. A review
Microchim. Acta

Bioelectron. 121 (2018) 265–271.
